question: Under Finnish FAS (KPL 5:6), what does the lower of cost and net realisable value rule mean for inventory valuation and what must a company do when NRV falls below cost?
gold_answer: Under Finnish FAS, specifically section 5:6 of the Accounting Act (KPL 5:6), the lower of cost and net realisable value rule means that inventory must be valued on the balance sheet at the lower of its original acquisition cost or its net realisable value (NRV) at the balance sheet date. Net realisable value is defined as the estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business, less any estimated costs necessary to make the sale. If the NRV of inventory falls below its acquisition cost, the company is required to write down the value of the inventory to its NRV, recognising the loss as an expense in the income statement. This ensures that inventory is not overstated on the balance sheet and that any expected losses are recognised promptly in the financial statements.

question: Saarinen Construction Oy acquired a subcontractor company for 680,000 euros. The net assets of the acquired company at the acquisition date were 420,000 euros. Under Finnish FAS, how is the 260,000 euro excess treated and what is the amortisation charge per year?
gold_answer: Under Finnish FAS, when Saarinen Construction Oy acquires a subcontractor company for 680,000 euros and the net assets at the acquisition date are 420,000 euros, the excess of 260,000 euros is recognised as goodwill (liikearvo) in the consolidated financial statements. Goodwill must be amortised on a straight-line basis over its useful economic life. If the useful life cannot be reliably estimated, the maximum amortisation period allowed is ten years. Therefore, if the maximum period is used, the annual amortisation charge for the 260,000 euros of goodwill would be 26,000 euros per year (260,000 euros divided by 10 years).

gold_answer: Under Finnish FAS and KILA instructions, the note disclosure for a business mortgage (yrityskiinnitys) pledged as security should show the nominal mortgage amount, not the outstanding loan balance. question: HelTech Solutions Oy capitalised 150,000 euros of software development costs during 2025. Under KPL 5:8 and KILA guidance, what conditions must have been satisfied for this treatment to be correct?
gold_answer: For HelTech Solutions Oy to correctly capitalise 150,000 euros of software development costs during 2025 under KPL 5:8 and KILA guidance, several conditions must be satisfied. The company must demonstrate that the development costs relate to an identifiable project that is expected to generate future economic benefits, such as a new software product or a significant enhancement to an existing system. The costs must be directly attributable to the development phase, meaning they are incurred after the project has passed the research stage and technical feasibility has been established. The company must also have the intention and resources to complete the development and use or sell the software. Furthermore, reliable measurement of the costs is required, and the capitalised amount must be recognised as an intangible asset in the balance sheet. If these conditions are not met, the costs should be expensed as incurred.

answer_result.answer:
Under the *kirjanpitolaki* (KPL) 5:8, *kehittämismenot* may be capitalised if they are expected to generate revenue over several accounting periods [Source 2]. The detailed conditions for capitalisation are set out in the *Työ- ja elinkeinoministeriön asetus kehittämismenojen aktivoinnista* (TEM decree), which implements the authorisation in KPL 5:8 [Source 1][Source 2]. For HelTech Solutions Oy’s €150,000 software development costs to be correctly capitalised, all six conditions in § 4 of the decree must be met: (1) the intangible asset is technically feasible to complete so that it can be used or sold; (2) the company intends to complete and use or sell it; (3) the company is able to use or sell it; (4) it is probable that the asset will generate future economic benefit; (5) the company has adequate technical, financial and other resources to complete or sell the development; and (6) the company can reliably measure the expenditure attributable to the asset during its development [Source 1].

Only costs directly caused by the development activity qualify as *kehittämismenot* under § 3 of the decree; costs of searching for, evaluating and selecting development projects are *tutkimusmenot*, which must be expensed immediately [Source 7]. Once capitalised, the costs must be amortised systematically over their useful life, and if the useful life cannot be reliably estimated, the maximum amortisation period is ten years [Source 2]. Furthermore, because HelTech Solutions Oy is an *osakeyhtiö*, § 8(3) of KPL 5:8 prohibits any distribution of the unamortised capitalised amount from the profit for the period, retained earnings or other distributable reserves [Source 2].

question: A Finnish group prepares consolidated accounts for the first time in 2025. The parent Oy Pohjoinen Ab owns 100% of an Austrian subsidiary. What consolidation eliminations are required under Finnish KPL consolidation rules?
gold_answer: When a Finnish group prepares consolidated accounts for the first time in 2025 and the parent company Oy Pohjoinen Ab owns 100% of an Austrian subsidiary, Finnish KPL consolidation rules require that the parent’s investment in the subsidiary and the subsidiary’s equity at the acquisition date are eliminated against each other to avoid double-counting. Any difference between the acquisition cost and the subsidiary’s net assets is recognised as goodwill or negative goodwill in the consolidated balance sheet. Intra-group receivables and liabilities, as well as intra-group income and expenses, must be eliminated so that only external transactions are reported. Additionally, any unrealised profits or losses from intra-group transactions, such as inventory or fixed asset transfers, must be eliminated to ensure the consolidated accounts reflect the group as a single economic entity and do not overstate profits or assets.

answer_result.answer:
Under KPL, the consolidation eliminations required for a Finnish parent (*Oy Pohjoinen Ab*) and its 100%-owned Austrian subsidiary are as follows. The consolidated financial statements must present the group’s result and financial position as if the group companies were a single accounting entity [Source 1]. Consequently, the effects of intra-group transactions must be eliminated [Source 1].

In the consolidated income statement, intra-group income and expenses, as well as internal profit distribution, must be deducted [Source 1][Source 2]. Additionally, changes during the financial period in intra-group profit margins that have been capitalized on the balance sheets of group companies must be deducted [Source 1].

Intra-group profit margins capitalized on the balance sheets of group companies must also be deducted [Source 1]. Furthermore, mutual share ownership must be eliminated [Source 2]. Since the parent owns 100% of the subsidiary, there is no minority interest to present as a separate item in the income statement or balance sheet [Source 1][Source 2].

Regarding the elimination of the subsidiary’s shares, the general rule is the acquisition cost method. However, an alternative pooling-of-interests method may be used if specific conditions are met, including that the parent holds at least nine-tenths of the subsidiary’s shares and the cash consideration paid was at most one-tenth of the nominal value of new shares issued by the parent as consideration [Source 3].

question: A UAE company receives interest payments of 200,000 euros per year from its Finnish subsidiary. The UAE has no corporate income tax. Finland has a tax treaty with the UAE. Explain how Finnish withholding tax applies to the interest payment, how the Finland-UAE treaty interacts with Finland's domestic interest withholding rules, and what OECD BEPS considerations are relevant.
gold_answer: When a UAE company receives interest payments of 200,000 euros per year from its Finnish subsidiary, Finnish withholding tax on the interest payment is determined by both Finland’s domestic tax law and the Finland-UAE tax treaty. Under Finnish domestic law, interest paid to a non-resident is generally not subject to withholding tax, except in certain cases such as payments to tax haven entities or in situations involving hybrid mismatches. However, since Finland has a tax treaty with the UAE, the treaty provisions take precedence over domestic law if they are more beneficial to the taxpayer. The Finland-UAE tax treaty typically either exempts interest from withholding tax or sets a maximum rate, and in most cases, interest paid to a UAE resident company is exempt from Finnish withholding tax under the treaty, provided the recipient is the beneficial owner of the interest and meets the treaty’s requirements. OECD BEPS (Base Erosion and Profit Shifting) considerations are also relevant, as Finland may deny treaty benefits or apply domestic anti-avoidance rules if the arrangement is considered artificial or if the UAE company is not the true beneficial owner of the interest. The principal purpose test (PPT) and other anti-abuse provisions in the treaty and Finnish law may be applied to prevent treaty shopping or the use of conduit companies. Therefore, while the Finland-UAE treaty generally exempts the interest payment from Finnish withholding tax, the exemption is subject to compliance with both treaty requirements and anti-avoidance rules designed to counteract BEPS risks .

question: Under the Finnish Tax Assessment Procedure Act and the Act on Self-Assessed Taxes, what are the reassessment periods for corporate income tax and VAT respectively, and how does the extended period for fraud apply?
gold_answer: Under the Finnish Tax Assessment Procedure Act, the standard reassessment period for corporate income tax is three years from the end of the tax year. question: A Finnish company licenses its software IP to a Swedish subsidiary for an annual royalty of 180,000 euros. Under the Finland-Sweden tax treaty, which country has the right to tax the royalty and what transfer pricing obligations arise for the Finnish company?

question: Koivunen Retail Oy receives a VAT reassessment decision from Vero for 2022 imposing a tax surcharge of 8,500 euros. What is the deadline to file a request for correction (oikaisuvaatimus) and what further appeal stages are available?
gold_answer: When Koivunen Retail Oy receives a VAT reassessment decision from Vero for 2022 that includes a tax surcharge of 8,500 euros, the company has 60 days from the date of service of the decision to file a request for correction (oikaisuvaatimus) with the Finnish Tax Administration. If necessary, a further appeal can be made to the Supreme Administrative Court (Korkein hallinto-oikeus), but this requires leave to appeal. question: An accounting firm stores client financial data including personal identification numbers, salary information, and tax returns on a cloud server. Under GDPR as implemented in Finland, what are the firm's key obligations as a data controller?
gold_answer: Under GDPR as implemented in Finland, an accounting firm that stores client financial data—including personal identification numbers, salary information, and tax returns—on a cloud server has several key obligations as a data controller. The firm must ensure that all personal data is processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently, and only for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes. It must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to safeguard the data, such as encryption, access controls, and secure storage, especially given the sensitive nature of the information. question: Finland introduced an enhanced R&D tax deduction in 2023 following a government proposal. What was the rationale and what are the key parameters of the measure as enacted?
gold_answer: The rationale behind Finland’s introduction of the enhanced R&D tax deduction in 2023 was to strengthen the country’s innovation capacity, boost productivity, and encourage private sector investment in research and development. The government aimed to make Finland more competitive internationally by providing stronger incentives for companies to increase their R&D activities, thereby supporting economic growth and technological advancement. The key parameters of the measure as enacted include a fixed additional deduction of 50% of qualifying R&D expenses, which companies can claim on top of the actual costs incurred. This additional deduction is available for in-house R&D activities and is capped at 500,000 euros per year. For collaborative R&D projects conducted with certain research organizations, a supplementary deduction of 45% is available, subject to its own cap. The enhanced deduction applies to qualifying R&D expenses as defined in the law, and companies must be able to document and substantiate their eligible costs. The measure is intended to be straightforward to apply and is designed to complement existing R&D incentives in the Finnish tax system.

answer_result.question: Finland introduced an enhanced R&D tax deduction in 2023 following a government proposal. What was the rationale and what are the key parameters of the measure as enacted?
answer_result.answer:
The enhanced R&D tax deduction introduced in 2023 is based on a legislative initiative (LA 69/2022 vp) and the related Finance Committee report (VaVM 35/2022 vp) [Source 4]. The rationale was to create a so-called *yhdistelmävähennys* (combined deduction) available to all taxpayers engaged in business or agricultural activities [Source 4]. The measure consists of two parts: a general additional deduction (*yleinen lisävähennys*) and an extra additional deduction (*ylimääräinen lisävähennys*) [Source 4].

The general additional deduction is 50 percent of qualifying R&D wage and purchased service expenses, with a minimum of €5,000 and a maximum of €500,000 per tax year [Source 4]. This general deduction can first be made for the 2023 tax year [Source 3][Source 4]. The extra additional deduction is 45 percent of the actual increase in R&D wage and purchased service expenses, also capped at €500,000 per tax year [Source 6]. However, the extra additional deduction is applied for the first time only for the 2024 tax year [Source 1].

The deduction cannot be made to the extent the taxpayer has received direct state aid or other public support for the qualifying expenses [Source 2]. Public support includes aid from entities such as Business Finland and ELY-keskukset, though loan financing for R&D is not considered such support [Source 2]. The law entered into force on 1 January 2023 and remains in force indefinitely [Source 1][Source 3].

question: Finland raised the standard VAT rate from 24% to 25.5% effective 1 September 2024. What transitional rules applied to goods and services supplied across the rate change date?
gold_answer: When Finland raised the standard VAT rate from 24% to 25.5% effective 1 September 2024, transitional rules were implemented to clarify how goods and services supplied across the rate change date should be treated. The key principle is that the applicable VAT rate is determined by the date when the goods are delivered or the service is performed, not by the invoice or payment date. If the supply of goods or the completion of a service occurred before 1 September 2024, the old 24% VAT rate applies, even if the invoice is issued or payment is received after that date. Conversely, if the supply or service is delivered on or after 1 September 2024, the new 25.5% VAT rate applies, regardless of when the contract was signed or the payment was made. For continuous services, such as leases or maintenance contracts, the supply is considered to take place at the end of each invoicing period, so the new rate applies to periods starting on or after 1 September 2024. These transitional rules ensure clarity and consistency in the application of the new VAT rate during the changeover period.

question: A Finnish company using the standard Tilikartta receives a supplier invoice for store rent of 8,000 euros plus 25.5% VAT. What accounts should be debited and credited to record this invoice?

question: In Finnish case law, how have the courts interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (liiketaloudelliset perusteet) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under EVL 52h§?
gold_answer: In Finnish case law, the courts have interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (liiketaloudelliset perusteet) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under EVL 52h§ as requiring that the restructuring is motivated by legitimate commercial objectives rather than tax avoidance. Genuine business reasons may include improving operational efficiency, facilitating business growth, separating different business activities, enhancing competitiveness, or responding to changes in market conditions. The courts have emphasised that restructurings must be planned and executed primarily for business purposes, such as streamlining management structures or preparing for expansion, and not solely to obtain tax benefits. If the restructuring is found to be driven mainly by tax motives, the tax-neutral treatment may be denied. The assessment is made on a case-by-case basis, considering the overall context and documentation provided by the company. answer_result.question: In Finnish case law, how have the courts interpreted the concept of genuine business reasons (liiketaloudelliset perusteet) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under EVL 52h§?
answer_result.answer:
In Finnish case law, the concept of genuine business reasons (*liiketaloudelliset perusteet*) in the context of tax-neutral corporate restructurings under *EVL* 52h § is assessed primarily through the lens of whether the arrangement creates concrete and systemically alien tax benefits, rather than through a direct weighing of the business reasons themselves. In KHO:2017:78, the Supreme Administrative Court (*KHO*) stated that because no such tax benefits were shown, "the business reasons presented in support of the arrangement do not need to be weighed further" [Source 1]. The court's focus is on the overall purpose of the arrangement; if a restructuring is merely an intermediate step in a scheme whose ultimate real purpose is a tax-driven sale, the *EVL* 52h § anti-avoidance provision may apply [Source 1]. However, a strong, tax-independent business reason can prevent the application of the general anti-avoidance rule in *VML* 28 §, as seen in KHO 2016:115, where the central reason for the demergers was a "strong business reason independent of taxation" [Source 2]. The *KHO* has also found that a demerger's purpose of selling a company constitutes a valid, tax-independent business reason, which precluded the application of the *VML* 28 § anti-avoidance rule to the subsequent dissolution of the companies when the arrangement was viewed as a whole [Source 5][Source 6][Source 7].

Huomioitavaa: Sources 5, 6, and 7 are marked as `repealed` and are derived from Vero guidance that is no longer in force. While they describe historical case law, their current interpretive authority is void.

question: The Finnish general anti-avoidance rule (veronkiertämissäännös) in VML 28§ can override technically lawful tax planning arrangements. What are the conditions for its application and how have the Finnish courts applied it?
gold_answer: The Finnish general anti-avoidance rule (veronkiertämissäännös) in VML 28§ allows the tax authorities to disregard the legal form of a transaction and recharacterise it for tax purposes if certain conditions are met, even if the arrangement is technically lawful. The key conditions for its application are that the arrangement must result in an unjustified tax benefit and that the main or one of the main purposes of the arrangement is to avoid tax. The rule applies when the legal structure of a transaction is artificial or does not correspond to its actual economic substance, and the arrangement is not motivated by genuine business reasons. Finnish courts have applied VML 28§ by examining the overall purpose and economic reality of the arrangement. If the courts find that the primary aim was to obtain a tax advantage without a legitimate commercial justification, they have upheld the tax authority’s right to disregard the arrangement and impose tax as if the arrangement had not been made. The courts assess factors such as the artificiality of the structure, the lack of real business purpose, and the presence of circular or contrived transactions. However, if the taxpayer can demonstrate genuine business reasons for the arrangement, the anti-avoidance rule is generally not applied. Each case is assessed individually, with a focus on substance over form and the intention behind the transaction.
gold_key_facts: The Finnish general anti-avoidance rule (veronkiertämissäännös) in VML 28§ allows the tax authorities to disregard the legal form of a transaction and recharacterise it for tax purposes if certain conditions are met, even if the arrangement is technically lawful.
The key conditions for its application are that the arrangement must result in an unjustified tax benefit and that the main or one of the main purposes of the arrangement is to avoid tax.
The rule applies when the legal structure of a transaction is artificial or does not correspond to its actual economic substance, and the arrangement is not motivated by genuine business reasons.

answer_result.question: The Finnish general anti-avoidance rule (veronkiertämissäännös) in VML 28§ can override technically lawful tax planning arrangements. What are the conditions for its application and how have the Finnish courts applied it?
answer_result.answer:
VML 28 §:n soveltaminen edellyttää kahta pääasiallista ehtoa: 1) oikeustoimelle on annettu oikeudellinen muoto, joka ei vastaa asian varsinaista luonnetta tai tarkoitusta, ja 2) järjestelyyn on ryhdytty ilmeisesti siinä tarkoituksessa, että suoritettavasta verosta vapauduttaisiin [Source 1][Source 4]. Säännöstä sovelletaan poikkeuksellisesti ja tapauskohtaisesti, ja sen käyttö edellyttää kokonaisarviointia, jossa punnitaan verovelvollisen toimien liiketaloudellisia perusteita suhteessa saavutettuun veroetuun [Source 2].

Korkein hallinto-oikeus on täsmentänyt, että VML 28 §:n sanamuoto asettaa kaksi ehtoa: järjestelyn oikeudellinen muoto ei vastaa sen tosiasiallista luonnetta ja tarkoitusta, ja järjestelyyn on ilmeisesti ryhdytty veronmaksun välttämistarkoituksessa. Mikäli järjestelylle on olemassa liiketaloudellinen peruste, ei järjestelyyn voida katsoa ryhdytyn veronvälttämistarkoituksessa [Source 4].

Oikeuskäytännössä säännöstä on sovellettu esimerkiksi tilanteessa *KHO 2017:20*, jossa kommandiittiyhtiön voitonjakolainan antajan tuottokorko vastasi lähes täyttä määrää yhtiön tuloksesta yhtiömiesten voitto-osuuden ollessa vain 250 euroa. Järjestelyä pidettiin poikkeuksellisena ja keinotekoisena, ja siihen katsottiin ryhdytyn ensi sijassa verotuksellisista syistä [Source 6].

Sen sijaan tapauksissa *KHO 2021:178*, *KHO 2022:79* ja *KHO 2017:78* säännöstä ei sovellettu, koska järjestelyille oli olemassa liiketaloudelliset perusteet tai veroetua ei pidetty lain tarkoituksen vastaisena. *KHO 2022:79*:ssä todettiin, ettei järjestelyssä syntynyt konkreettisia ja järjestelmälle vieraita veroetuja, ja *KHO 2016:115*:n mukaan VML 28 §:ää ei sovelleta, kun jakautumisten keskeisenä syynä on verotuksesta riippumaton vahva liiketaloudellinen syy [Source 4][Source 7][Source 8].

*KHO 2025:37* korosti, ettei VML 28 §:ää voida soveltaa siten, että sulautumista kohdeltaisiin verotuksessa muutoin sulautumisena, mutta tappioiden ei katsottaisi siirtyvän, kun tappiot siirtyvät suoraan lain nojalla. Tällainen soveltaminen tarkoittaisi jotain muuta kuin oikeudellisen muodon sivuuttamista [Source 5].
